children.value: In this section, we will learn about branches in Git. We will discuss why they are useful, what they are, how to create them, and how to switch between them.

Branches allow you to diverge from the main line of development and work independently without affecting the main line. This is useful when you want to work on a new feature or fix a bug without affecting the main line of development. Once you are done with your work, you can combine your changes back into the main line.

A branch is a lightweight movable pointer to a commit. When Git initializes a repository, it creates a branch. As you commit changes, Git moves the branch pointer forward to point to the latest commit. In our repository, we used the option
to indicate that the initial branch should be named

state). It indicates the “current” snapshot of your repository that you are working on. Git stores the
Let’s add some commits to our commit history to see how the branch pointer moves. Let’s add a new line to our
file and check the status of the repository.

echo "This is the second line." >> file1.txt
git status
You need to save your changes for Git to track them.
VSC adds a dot to the file names to indicate unsaved changes.
This time, instead of using the command line, we will use VSC to stage the changes.
: In VSC, click on the Source Control icon on the sidebar or press
on macOS) to open the Source Control panel.
: You’ll see a list of changed files in the Source Control panel. These files are modifications in your working directory that Git has not yet committed.
To stage an individual file, hover over the file name and click the
icon. This action stages that specific file.
To stage all changes, click the
icon next to the “Changes” section at the top of the list. This stages all modified files.
Now that we have staged changes, we can commit them.
: At the top of the Source Control panel, you’ll find a text field to enter your commit message. Enter “Add second line to file1.txt” as the commit message.
To commit the staged changes, click the checkmark icon (
) above the text field where you entered your commit message. Alternatively, you can press
git commit -m "Add second line to file1.txt"
Checking how the branch pointer moves

children.label: Checking how the branch pointer moves

children.value: Let’s check the commit history again to see how the branch pointer moves.
The branch automatically moves to point to the latest commit.

```{code-cell} bash
git cat-file -p main
```
```{admonition} What to notice
:class: hint
The commit keeps a pointer to its parent, linking the commit history.
```

As the number of commits grows, it becomes harder to keep track of the commit history. This is where representing the commit history with a diagram comes in handy. In our diagram, we will represent commits as circles, branches with boxes and the

flowchart RL
    C2((C2)) --> C1((C1))
    C3((C3)) --> C2
    main --> C2
    feature --> C3
    HEAD{HEAD} --> feature
